On this page

E-Communication ​

Electronic communication has replaced letters, fax machines, and phone trees. Email, instant messaging, video conferencing, and social media all rely on the same underlying networks — but they come with unique protocols, etiquette requirements, and threats.

Grade 10–11

E-communication is introduced at Grade 10 (email basics, netiquette, threats) and extended at Grade 11 (VoIP, RSS, protocols).

Forms of Electronic Communication ​

TypeDescriptionExamples
EmailAsynchronous text/file communication via mail serversGmail, Outlook
Instant messaging (IM)Real-time text chatWhatsApp, Telegram, Teams
VoIPVoice (and video) calls over the internetZoom, Teams, WhatsApp calls, Skype
Social mediaSharing content, updates, and communicationFacebook, Instagram, X (Twitter)
RSS feedsSubscribing to website updatesNews aggregators, podcasts
Discussion forumsAsynchronous threaded discussionsReddit, Stack Overflow
Blog / vlogPublishing written or video contentWordPress, YouTube
Video conferencingReal-time multi-party video callsZoom, Google Meet, Teams

Email — How it Works ​

Email relies on three protocols:

ProtocolFull NamePurpose
SMTPSimple Mail Transfer ProtocolSending email (port 25/587)
POP3Post Office Protocol v3Downloading email from server (port 110) — deletes from server
IMAPInternet Message Access ProtocolAccessing email on server (port 143) — stays on server

SMTP Process (sending): ​

  1. You compose and send an email in your email client
  2. Client connects to your outgoing mail server (SMTP)
  3. SMTP server looks up the recipient domain's MX record via DNS
  4. Connects to the recipient's mail server and delivers the message
  5. Message sits in the recipient's mailbox until they download/view it

POP3 vs IMAP ​

FeaturePOP3IMAP
Email storedDownloaded to deviceStays on server
Multiple devicesPoor — one device at a timeExcellent — syncs across all devices
Offline accessYes (after download)Limited (needs connection to view)
StorageLocal deviceServer storage
Best forSingle deviceMultiple devices

Email Best Practices ​

Structure of a professional email: ​

  • To — primary recipient(s)
  • CC (Carbon Copy) — receives a copy; all recipients can see this
  • BCC (Blind Carbon Copy) — receives a copy; other recipients cannot see BCC list
  • Subject — clear, specific summary of the email topic
  • Body — professional, clear language
  • Signature — name, position, contact details

Attachment guidelines: ​

  • Keep file sizes small — compress large attachments
  • Warn recipient before sending large files
  • Never open unexpected attachments from unknown senders

Netiquette ​

Netiquette (internet etiquette) — rules for respectful and appropriate online communication.

RuleExplanation
No caps lockTYPING IN ALL CAPS = shouting — considered rude
Use clear subject linesHelps recipient prioritise and find email later
Reply promptlyRespond within 24–48 hours for professional email
Don't forward without permissionPrivate emails should not be forwarded
Proofread before sendingCheck spelling, tone, and correct recipients
Don't CC unnecessarilyOnly CC people who need to be informed
Respect others' timeBe concise — don't send unnecessarily long emails
No spamDon't send unsolicited bulk email
Cite sourcesIf sharing information, credit the original author
Be respectfulOnline anonymity doesn't excuse rude behaviour

VoIP — Voice over Internet Protocol ​

VoIP converts voice (and video) into digital data packets and transmits them over the internet.

FeatureDetail
How it worksAudio → digitised → compressed → sent as data packets → reassembled
AdvantageCheap or free calls anywhere in the world
DisadvantageQuality depends on internet connection; latency can cause echo
ExamplesZoom, Microsoft Teams, WhatsApp calls, Skype, Google Meet

VoIP vs Traditional phone:

VoIPTraditional
CostLow (uses existing internet)Higher (phone network charges)
International callsCheap/freeExpensive
QualityVaries with internetConsistent
Infrastructure neededInternet connectionPhone line

RSS — Really Simple Syndication ​

RSS is a format that allows users to subscribe to updates from websites and have new content delivered automatically.

  • Website publishes an RSS feed (XML file) that updates when new content is added
  • User subscribes in an RSS reader (aggregator)
  • New articles, podcasts, or news items appear in the reader automatically
  • No need to visit each website individually

Examples: news aggregators, podcast subscriptions, blog updates

Email Threats ​

ThreatDescription
SpamUnsolicited bulk email — usually advertising or malicious
PhishingFake email pretending to be a trusted organisation to steal credentials
Spear phishingTargeted phishing aimed at a specific person (more convincing)
VishingVoice phishing — scam phone calls
SmishingSMS phishing — fake text messages
Email spoofingForging the sender address to appear legitimate
Malicious attachmentsEmail attachments containing malware
Malicious linksLinks that redirect to fake or infected websites

Identifying phishing emails: ​

  • Urgent or threatening language ("Your account will be suspended!")
  • Sender email address doesn't match claimed organisation
  • Hover over links — URL doesn't match display text
  • Generic greeting ("Dear Customer" instead of your name)
  • Requests for passwords, PINs, or banking details
  • Grammar and spelling errors

Protection Against Email Threats ​

MeasureHow it helps
Spam filterAutomatically moves suspicious email to junk/spam
AntivirusScans attachments before opening
2FA on email accountsEven if password is stolen, account can't be accessed
Don't click unknown linksVisit websites directly by typing the URL
Verify requestsPhone the organisation directly to confirm requests
Keep software updatedPatches vulnerabilities exploited by malware
Check sender addressLook at actual email address, not just display name

Online Communication and Privacy ​

IssueConsideration
Digital footprintAll online activity leaves traces — emails, posts, chats
Cloud storage of messagesEmails on cloud servers can be accessed by provider
End-to-end encryptionWhatsApp, Signal — only sender/receiver can read
Data retentionMany services keep your messages for years

Key Terms ​

TermDefinition
EmailElectronic message sent via mail servers
SMTPProtocol for sending email
POP3Protocol for downloading email (removes from server)
IMAPProtocol for accessing email while keeping it on server
CCCarbon copy — sends a copy; visible to all recipients
BCCBlind carbon copy — hidden from other recipients
VoIPVoice over IP — voice/video calls over the internet
RSSFormat for subscribing to website content updates
NetiquetteEtiquette rules for online communication
SpamUnsolicited bulk email
PhishingFake emails designed to steal credentials
SpoofingForging sender identity to appear legitimate

Exam Focus ​

High-Frequency Questions

  1. "Explain the difference between POP3 and IMAP" — POP3 downloads email to one device and deletes from server; IMAP keeps email on the server and syncs across multiple devices

  2. "What is VoIP? Give ONE advantage and ONE disadvantage" — Voice over IP — converts voice to data packets sent over internet; advantage: cheap/free international calls; disadvantage: quality depends on internet connection

  3. "Give FOUR rules of netiquette" — don't type in all caps; use clear subject lines; don't forward without permission; reply promptly; be concise

  4. "How can you identify a phishing email?" — urgent language; sender address doesn't match; hover over links to see real URL; generic greeting; requests for passwords/banking details; spelling errors

  5. "What is the difference between phishing and spear phishing?" — phishing is a mass email to many people; spear phishing is targeted at a specific individual and is more convincing as it uses personal details
